Results against success criteria (high level)
- Detection + failover time:
  - First corroborated impairment signal observed: ~T+75s from impairment start.
  - Policy flip executed: ~T+2m40s.
  - Traffic settled in eu-west: ~T+4–5m (client retry/backoff dominated tail).
- Error/latency impact:
  - Hosted API: short spike in 5xx/timeouts during the shift window; elevated tail latency in eu-west for ~12–18 minutes as retries + warm-up converged.
  - Dedicated: no unplanned customer pool drain; no sustained SLO-relevant impact observed.
- Safety/guardrails:
  - No uncontrolled oscillation, but we did see an automated rollback trigger earlier than intended due to hold-down/threshold interaction (details below).

Customer impact / support signals
- One customer reported transient 5xx/timeouts during the planned window (SUP-25310).
- Streaming disconnects were reported by a couple customers whose clients did not gracefully reconnect on redirect (SUP-25322).
- Follow-on ticket on elevated tail latency in eu-west immediately post-shift (SUP-25341) — correlates with headroom + retry storm.

What went well
- On-call coordination and go/no-go discipline were strong (checklist completion and rollback readiness were clear).
- Region health aggregation + policy engine evaluation produced explainable decisions (we could point to which signals tripped).
- Dedicated isolation worked as expected (Hosted shift did not inadvertently trigger Dedicated drain behaviors).

Issues found (key)
1) Observability gap: dashboards lacked consistent automated annotations for: impairment start, policy flip, rollback evaluation points. This made timeline reconstruction slower than it should be.
2) Anti-flap / rollback tuning: hold-down timer and corroboration windows were too aggressive in one segment, resulting in an early rollback evaluation while the region was still stabilizing.
3) Tail latency in eu-west after shift: headroom assumptions were optimistic for the specific routes/models in scope; combined with client retry behavior this caused a brief tail spike.
4) Streaming client behavior: redirect/reconnect handling still causes user-visible disconnects for some SDK versions / client patterns.

Decisions / next steps
- We are NOT expanding automation scope until the above items are closed and we pass a smaller-blast-radius rehearsal.
- We will schedule Game Day #2 with tighter allowlist + synthetic load shaping, and explicitly measure: failover time, retry amplification, and “time to steady-state p99”.

Action items (short list; full tracker linked)
- Add automation-driven dashboard annotations + incident timeline hooks (Owner: Sean / Observability; target: end of Jan)
- Retune rollback/hold-down thresholds and add “stabilization grace” logic (Owner: Rafael + Routing; target: end of Jan)
- Capacity guardrail: require minimum eu-west headroom for routes in allowlist before executing flip (Owner: Irene + Fleet; target: early Feb)
- Streaming: finalize redirect-safe reconnect behavior + publish SDK guidance (Owner: Priya + Runtime/Gateway; target: early Feb)
- Comms: align Support macros + status-page language for planned tests (Owner: Sean; target: before next test)

Links
- Results page (metrics + timeline): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Controlled+Failover+Test+Results+-+2026-01-15
- Draft postmortem (focus on regressions + prevention): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Postmortem%3A+Controlled+Failover+Test+Regression
- Action item tracker (ENG-2426): https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-2426/post-gameday-action-items

Asks
1) Approval to hold Game Day #2 in early Feb (exact date TBD) once the above gating items are closed.
2) Confirmation that we should proactively notify the same set of enterprise accounts as last time (Support has the list), or expand/contract based on risk appetite.

Thanks to everyone who helped make the test controlled and safe — we learned the right things without a large blast radius.

— Ava
Ava Chen
Infrastructure Reliability
Redwood Inference

Subject: Re: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)

Thanks, Ava.

A couple questions before I approve another production test window:
1) Were there any indications we violated (or came close to violating) the multi-region availability SLO budget during the test window?
2) What is our plan to ensure the next test doesn’t create a retry amplification event in the target region? (I want this explicitly called out in go/no-go.)

On Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 12:05 PM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 17, 2026 at 12:05 PM PT
Subject: Re: Post-game day summary + follow-ups (Controlled regional failover us-east → eu-west, 1/15)

Answers inline.

1) SLO/error budget proximity
- We did not materially burn the multi-region availability SLO budget for the month.
- The spike was brief and scoped; the rolling burn-rate alerts triggered as expected, but did not cross paging thresholds for sustained windows.
- We’ll include the exact numbers in the postmortem once the annotation gap is fixed (so we can line up the windows precisely), but this was within the planned “acceptable test impact” bounds.

2) Preventing retry amplification in the target region (explicit go/no-go)
We’re adding three concrete gates before we run Game Day #2:
- Capacity/headroom gate: require minimum eu-west headroom for the exact route/model allowlist (not just aggregate regional headroom). Irene’s team is helping define thresholds.
- Gateway/backoff gate: validate retry budgets and Retry-After behavior in staging under redirect (Priya’s team + Peter’s circuit breaker changes), with a target “max retry amplification” metric.
- Synthetic load rehearsal: replay a shaped load pattern with perf canaries enabled and confirm p99 stabilization time is within target before touching prod.

If those three are green, we’ll propose an early Feb date with an even smaller allowlist than 1/15.

I’ll also make sure the go/no-go doc includes an explicit rollback trigger tied to retry amplification (not just error burn).

— Ava
Ava Chen
Infrastructure Reliability
Redwood Inference

TL;DR
- The core automation path worked end-to-end (signal → controller decision → routing policy flip → traffic shift → stabilization).
- Customer impact was limited but not zero: brief elevated 5xx/timeouts for a small slice of Hosted traffic + streaming disconnects for some clients that don’t handle redirects cleanly.
- We found several “must-fix before wider rollout” items (anti-flap tuning, dashboard annotations, streaming reconnect guidance, and capacity headroom guardrails).

What we tested
- Scenario: planned impairment simulation in us-east and controlled failover to eu-west for a scoped set of routes/models and a limited tenant allowlist.

  - Dedicated: no unplanned customer pool drain; no sustained SLO-relevant impact observed.
- Safety/guardrails:

- Dedicated isolation worked as expected (Hosted shift did not inadvertently trigger Dedicated drain behaviors).

Issues found (key)
1) Observability gap: dashboards lacked consistent automated annotations for: impairment start, policy flip, rollback evaluation points. This made timeline reconstruction slower than it should be.
2) Anti-flap / rollback tuning: hold-down timer and corroboration windows were too aggressive in one segment, resulting in an early rollback evaluation while the region was still stabilizing.

4) Streaming client behavior: redirect/reconnect handling still causes user-visible disconnects for some SDK versions / client patterns.

- Streaming: finalize redirect-safe reconnect behavior + publish SDK guidance (Owner: Priya + Runtime/Gateway; target: early Feb)
- Comms: align Support macros + status-page language for planned tests (Owner: Sean; target: before next test)

Links

- Draft postmortem (focus on regressions + prevention): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ONCALL/Postmortem%3A+Controlled+Failover+Test+Regression
- Action item tracker (ENG-2426): https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-2426/post-gameday-action-items

Asks

2) Confirmation that we should proactively notify the same set of enterprise accounts as last time (Support has the list), or expand/contract based on risk appetite.

related_links: Linear: PM-481 (Quality guardrails gating PRD + launch plan)
Linear: ENG-3928 (Rollout controller: block promotion on gate failure)
GitHub: redwood/pr-19531 (Block apply when gate fails + surface reasons)
GitHub: redwood/pr-19537 (Override flow w/ expiration + audit events)
source.body:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19
Attachments: Optimize_Quality_Gates_Limited_Beta_Approval_Checklist_v3.pdf; limited-beta-route-list_2026-01-14.csv

Ava — requesting final leadership approval to start the limited beta for Optimize Quality Gates on hosted API routes the week of Jan 19.

What’s shipping in limited beta
- Enforce “quality gates required” for Optimize-suggested config diffs (batching policy, prefix/KV cache settings, quantization profile changes)
- Gate tiers:
  - Fast smoke gate first (small prompt subset) to catch obvious regressions quickly
  - Full gate required before any canary promotion to 100%
- Enforcement (defense-in-depth):
  - Optimize “Apply” endpoint blocks when gate = failed / incomplete
  - Rollout controller blocks canary promotion on gate failure and triggers rollback hooks
- Audit trail:
  - Gate run started/completed + pass/fail reasons
  - Override granted w/ expiration and reason

Proposed schedule
- Thu 1/16: enable gating enforcement in prod behind allowlist (routes/accounts only)
- Mon 1/19–Wed 1/21: onboard first 3 routes (internal + one friendly customer)
- Thu 1/22: expand to remaining allowlisted routes if pass rate + eval runtime are within bounds

Beta scope
- Hosted API only
- Allowlist routes/accounts in attached CSV (no auto-apply; one-click apply only)
- Explicit opt-in from CS for any external customer route

Sign-offs (collected offline; summarized in attached checklist)
- Applied ML (metrics/thresholds): approved pending flake monitoring + minimum sample sizes
- SRE (SLO/alerting): approved pending “gate execution latency” dashboard + alert thresholds
- Runtime (safety/knobs): approved w/ static validation pre-checks + guardrails on risky quant profile combos

Ask
- Can you reply with “Approved” (or conditions), so we can proceed with the 1/16 enablement and communicate to PM/SRE/Runtime?

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Product Lead, Optimize
Redwood Inference


---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 10:05 AM PST
Subject: Re: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19

A few checks before I sign off:

1) Overrides: who can approve during beta, and how are we preventing “rubber stamping” given the near-miss last month?
2) Is auto-apply fully disabled for allowlisted routes? I don’t want any background jobs applying changes without an explicit human in the loop.
3) Rollout coupling: is the rollout controller also enforcing, or are we relying only on Optimize API blocking?
4) What’s our go/no-go criteria for the 1/22 expansion (pass rate, flake rate, gate runtime)?

Send me answers + any conditions you want to attach, and I’ll respond with approval.

— Ava


---

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 11:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19

Answers inline.

1) Overrides
- Beta policy: overrides are allowed but time-bound (24h default; 72h max) and must include (a) reason code, (b) linked evidence/artifacts, (c) owner + follow-up ticket.
- Approvers (beta): oncall E7+ in Eng + one designated Product approver (me). No “self-approve” (requester != approver enforced).
- Every override emits an audit event and shows up in Console’s audit view; we’ll do a daily review during beta.
- If you want to tighten further, we can start with “no overrides for quantization profile changes” during week 1.

2) Auto-apply
- Confirmed: auto-apply is disabled for allowlisted routes for the duration of the limited beta. Optimize will still generate recommendations, but applying requires one-click + gate pass.

3) Enforcement location
- Both:
  - Optimize API blocks apply if gates failed/incomplete/expired
  - Rollout controller blocks canary promotion if gate != passed and will halt rollout + trigger rollback policy when configured
This is explicitly to avoid relying on a single enforcement point.

4) Go/no-go criteria for 1/22 expansion
Proposed thresholds (happy to adjust):
- Gate runtime P95: smoke <= 12 min; full <= 60 min (for the allowlisted routes)
- Flake rate: <= 3% (where flake = requires rerun due to variance / non-deterministic scoring)
- Gate pass rate: >= 85% for “safe” diffs (batching/cache). For quantization, expect lower initially; expansion depends on distribution.
- Zero incidents: no production canary promotion occurs with failed gates; no observed silent quality regression in online signals attributable to a gated change

If you’re OK with it, I’ll add “no overrides for quant changes in week 1” as a condition to keep risk low.

Aditya


---

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 12:18 PM PST
Subject: Re: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19

Approved with conditions:

- Limited beta only (allowlist as attached), hosted API only.
- Auto-apply remains disabled through the limited beta.
- Week 1: no overrides for quantization profile changes. If we need an exception, escalate to me with artifacts.
- Daily report (Mon–Fri) to me: gate run counts, pass/fail breakdown, flake rate, P95 runtime (smoke + full), and any overrides/audit entries.
- 1/22 expansion requires meeting your thresholds AND an explicit go/no-go check with SRE on gate latency + regional capacity.

Please make sure the rollout comms call out what happens on gate failures (blocked apply + rollout halt) so CS isn’t surprised.

— Ava


---

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, Jan 15, 2026 at 8:41 AM PST
Subject: Re: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19

Confirmed on all conditions.

I’ll:
- Keep auto-apply disabled for the allowlist through the limited beta
- Enforce “no overrides for quant profile changes” in week 1 (override request path will hard-block with a message to escalate)
- Send the daily report starting Monday 1/19
- Schedule a 15-min go/no-go on Thu 1/22 with SRE for runtime/region capacity + thresholds review

Re: CS comms — agreed. I’ll update the internal beta notes today with:
- What users see when a gate fails (blocked apply, reasons, artifact links)
- What happens during rollout (canary halt, rollback hooks)
- How to request an override (and that quant overrides are disabled week 1)

Thanks,
Aditya

- Approvers (beta): oncall E7+ in Eng + one designated Product approver (me). No “self-approve” (requester != approver enforced).
- Every override emits an audit event and shows up in Console’s audit view; we’ll do a daily review during beta.
- If you want to tighten further, we can start with “no overrides for quantization profile changes” during week 1.

2) Auto-apply
- Confirmed: auto-apply is disabled for allowlisted routes for the duration of the limited beta. Optimize will still generate recommendations, but applying requires one-click + gate pass.

3) Enforcement location
- Both:
  - Optimize API blocks apply if gates failed/incomplete/expired
  - Rollout controller blocks canary promotion if gate != passed and will halt rollout + trigger rollback policy when configured
This is explicitly to avoid relying on a single enforcement point.

4) Go/no-go criteria for 1/22 expansion
Proposed thresholds (happy to adjust):
- Gate runtime P95: smoke <= 12 min; full <= 60 min (for the allowlisted routes)
- Flake rate: <= 3% (where flake = requires rerun due to variance / non-deterministic scoring)
- Gate pass rate: >= 85% for “safe” diffs (batching/cache). For quantization, expect lower initially; expansion depends on distribution.
- Zero incidents: no production canary promotion occurs with failed gates; no observed silent quality regression in online signals attributable to a gated change

If you’re OK with it, I’ll add “no overrides for quant changes in week 1” as a condition to keep risk low.

Aditya

To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 12:18 PM PST
Subject: Re: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19

Approved with conditions:

- Limited beta only (allowlist as attached), hosted API only.
- Auto-apply remains disabled through the limited beta.
- Week 1: no overrides for quantization profile changes. If we need an exception, escalate to me with artifacts.
- Daily report (Mon–Fri) to me: gate run counts, pass/fail breakdown, flake rate, P95 runtime (smoke + full), and any overrides/audit entries.
- 1/22 expansion requires meeting your thresholds AND an explicit go/no-go check with SRE on gate latency + regional capacity.

Please make sure the rollout comms call out what happens on gate failures (blocked apply + rollout halt) so CS isn’t surprised.

— Ava

- Schedule a 15-min go/no-go on Thu 1/22 with SRE for runtime/region capacity + thresholds review

Aditya
text:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, Jan 14, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Limited beta approvals: Optimize Quality Gates (hosted API) — rollout week of Jan 19
Attachments: Optimize_Quality_Gates_Limited_Beta_Approval_Checklist_v3.pdf; limited-beta-route-list_2026-01-14.csv

Ava — requesting final leadership approval to start the limited beta for Optimize Quality Gates on hosted API routes the week of Jan 19.

What’s shipping in limited beta
- Enforce “quality gates required” for Optimize-suggested config diffs (batching policy, prefix/KV cache settings, quantization profile changes)
- Gate tiers:
  - Fast smoke gate first (small prompt subset) to catch obvious regressions quickly
  - Full gate required before any canary promotion to 100%
- Enforcement (defense-in-depth):
  - Optimize “Apply” endpoint blocks when gate = failed / incomplete
  - Rollout controller blocks canary promotion on gate failure and triggers rollback hooks
- Audit trail:
  - Gate run started/completed + pass/fail reasons
  - Override granted w/ expiration and reason

Proposed schedule
- Thu 1/16: enable gating enforcement in prod behind allowlist (routes/accounts only)
- Mon 1/19–Wed 1/21: onboard first 3 routes (internal + one friendly customer)
- Thu 1/22: expand to remaining allowlisted routes if pass rate + eval runtime are within bounds

Beta scope
- Hosted API only
- Allowlist routes/accounts in attached CSV (no auto-apply; one-click apply only)
- Explicit opt-in from CS for any external customer route

Sign-offs (collected offline; summarized in attached checklist)
- Applied ML (metrics/thresholds): approved pending flake monitoring + minimum sample sizes
- SRE (SLO/alerting): approved pending “gate execution latency” dashboard + alert thresholds
- Runtime (safety/knobs): approved w/ static validation pre-checks + guardrails on risky quant profile combos

Ask
- Can you reply with “Approved” (or conditions), so we can proceed with the 1/16 enablement and communicate to PM/SRE/Runtime?

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Product Lead, Optimize
Redwood Inference


---

4) What’s our go/no-go criteria for the 1/22 expansion (pass rate, flake rate, gate runtime)?

Send me answers + any conditions you want to attach, and I’ll respond with approval.

— Ava

source.subject: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)
source.body:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 12, 2026 at 8:17 AM PST
Subject: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)

Hi Neha,

Sharing a short exec readout on the “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” work as we start the first full sweep week. The team is on track on harness/workloads, but we’re bottlenecked on variance + benchmark fleet capacity stability.

Attachment: Q1-2026-Perf-Baseline-Exec-Readout-v1.pdf
Attachment: benchmark-cluster-capacity-plan-q1-2026.xlsx

Summary status (as of Jan 12)
- Representative workload suite v1: implemented for chat streaming, long-context prefill, embeddings. Tool-calling workload is next and not blocking the first baseline freeze.
- Perf harness: run manifest validator is merged + wired into CI for the benchmark repo; we can now attribute changes to driver/runtime/scheduler/kernel selection reliably.
- Baseline sweep: first partial sweep completed (us-east only) for top two model families on H100. eu-west runs are blocked by fleet availability and some IAM friction.

Key risks
1) Variance/noise is still too high to set regression thresholds confidently
   - We’re seeing ~6–12% p99 TTFT swing run-to-run on long-prefill on shared nodes (most correlated with GPU clock variability + co-tenancy).
   - Warmup is now standardized, but we still need consistent isolation (or a “benchmark mode” scheduling window).
   - Without addressing this, regression gating will either page constantly or be too lax.

2) Benchmark fleet capacity and access
   - Current reservation model is “best effort,” leading to missed windows and mixed hardware (especially for eu-west).
   - IAM/service account permissions for artifact export + node-level controls are not fully unblocked (tracking in INT-8821).

3) Timeline risk to baseline freeze
   - If we can’t stabilize variance and secure dedicated windows, we risk slipping the baseline anchor date by ~1–2 weeks, which cascades into release gating adoption.

Asks / decisions needed this week
A) Approve dedicated benchmark windows (2 weeks) for baseline freeze
   - H100: us-east + eu-west (priority)
   - A100/L40S: us-east (secondary; can be staggered)
   - Goal: consistent hardware + isolation to lock the “golden” Q1 baseline numbers.

B) Approve incremental GPU-hour budget buffer
   - We need headroom for reruns while calibrating thresholds (noise model needs more samples per cohort).
   - See attached capacity plan sheet; the ask is to approve the “calibration buffer” line item.

C) Support priority to unblock access + stability
   - Fast-track INT-8821 (service account + node controls).
   - Align on whether we can temporarily pause lower-priority internal GPU consumers during reserved windows.

If helpful, I can do a 15-minute walkthrough. If you’re aligned, I’ll ask the team to treat Jan 24 as the tentative “baseline anchor build” date, contingent on the reservation + IAM items.

Thanks,
Ava

—
Ava Chen
Director, Infrastructure Programs
Redwood Inference


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 12, 2026 at 9:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)

Thanks, Ava.

On the asks:
- Dedicated windows: Approved in principle. Please send me the exact SKU x region window proposal (dates/times) and I’ll have SRE/infra enforce the reservation blocks.
- Budget buffer: Approved for the calibration buffer in the attached sheet. Finance doesn’t need extra ceremony for this; just keep it to the plan.
- INT-8821: I’ll ping the IAM/infra owner today to get this unblocked. If it’s not resolved by EOD tomorrow, escalate back to me and we’ll treat it as a stop-the-line issue for the baseline work.

Re: pausing other internal GPU consumers — I’m OK doing this during reserved windows if we can communicate 48 hours ahead and limit to 2 weeks.

Let’s do 15 minutes tomorrow. Include the proposed baseline anchor date and what “done” means for the freeze.

Neha

—
Neha Kapoor
VP, Infrastructure & Reliability
Redwood Inference


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 12, 2026 at 10:11 AM PST
Subject: Re: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)

Great — thank you.

Proposed reservation blocks (baseline freeze sprint)
- us-east / H100: Jan 13–Jan 24, 6pm–6am PT (nightly), plus Sat Jan 17 full-day for the long-prefill cohorts
- eu-west / H100: Jan 14–Jan 24, local overnight windows (we can match 7pm–7am CET)
- us-east / A100: Jan 19–Jan 23, 6pm–6am PT (only after H100 cohorts are stable)

Definition of baseline freeze “done” (for Jan 24 anchor)
- For each baseline cohort (model family x workload x region x SKU):
  - p50/p95/p99 for TTFT and TPOT with stable variance bands
  - throughput (tokens/sec and req/sec where applicable)
  - run manifest + env capture stored and queryable
  - cold vs warm cache scenarios recorded (decision already made to report both)
- Noise model calibrated enough to set initial gating thresholds (with “warn” vs “fail” separation)

For the 15-minute tomorrow: I’ll bring a 1-slide view of current variance drivers and what we expect to change with isolation/clock controls.

Also, re: INT-8821 — the immediate pain is artifact export + node-level settings for clock locking. If we can’t get clock locking, we can still run, but the variance bands widen and gating becomes less useful.

Thanks again,
Ava


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 12, 2026 at 2:06 PM PST
Subject: Re: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)

Approved on those blocks.

Action items:
1) Send the reservation blocks to SRE scheduling + benchmark fleet owner today (cc me on the note).
2) Put the “48-hour heads up” comms in place for the internal GPU pause windows (keep it tight and time-bound).
3) For tomorrow’s 15 min: please include a recommendation on the minimum acceptable variance to start gating (even if we start in “warn-only” mode).

Re: clock locking — I agree it’s worth doing for the baseline suite; let’s treat it as required for H100 baseline runs.

Neha

For the 15-minute tomorrow: I’ll bring a 1-slide view of current variance drivers and what we expect to change with isolation/clock controls.

Also, re: INT-8821 — the immediate pain is artifact export + node-level settings for clock locking. If we can’t get clock locking, we can still run, but the variance bands widen and gating becomes less useful.

Thanks again,
Ava


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 12, 2026 at 2:06 PM PST
Subject: Re: Q1’26 Perf Baseline — exec readout + asks (variance + benchmark fleet capacity)

Approved on those blocks.

Action items:
1) Send the reservation blocks to SRE scheduling + benchmark fleet owner today (cc me on the note).
2) Put the “48-hour heads up” comms in place for the internal GPU pause windows (keep it tight and time-bound).
3) For tomorrow’s 15 min: please include a recommendation on the minimum acceptable variance to start gating (even if we start in “warn-only” mode).

Re: clock locking — I agree it’s worth doing for the baseline suite; let’s treat it as required for H100 baseline runs.

Neha

source.subject: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions
source.body:
From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 9:12 AM
Subject: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions
Attachments: optimization-audit-events-v1.0.docx, audit-log-payload-examples-optimization.txt

Marissa — completed security review of the Optimization Config audit event taxonomy (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback). Overall direction looks good, but we need a few changes before I can sign off for Limited GA.

Top feedback (required):

1) Event naming + lifecycle coverage
- Please standardize to a single namespace and tense. Proposed:
  - optimization_config.version_created
  - optimization_config.version_validated
  - optimization_config.rollout_started
  - optimization_config.rollout_paused
  - optimization_config.rollout_gate_failed
  - optimization_config.rollout_promoted
  - optimization_config.rollback_requested
  - optimization_config.rollback_completed
- We need BOTH “requested” and “completed” for rollback. “Completed” must reflect actual control-plane state change to prior active version (not just that an operator clicked a button).

2) Required fields (minimum) for enterprise auditability
For every event above, please ensure these are ALWAYS present (even on failures):
- event_id (uuid), event_time (RFC3339), event_name, event_schema_version
- actor_type (human | service), actor_id (user id / service id), actor_email (if human), actor_ip, user_agent (if available)
- authz_decision (allow/deny) and authz_policy_version (so we can prove RBAC enforcement)
- request_id and trace_id (or equivalent correlation IDs)
- environment (hosted | dedicated | private)
- tenant_id
- scope_model (model id/name), scope_region, scope_deployment_id (if applicable)
- config_object_id (Optimization Config object), config_version_id (immutable version), and previous_active_version_id (where applicable)
- result (success|failure) + error_code/error_summary (for failure)

3) “Blast radius” + intent
For apply/promote/rollback events, we need fields that capture what was intended:
- rollout_id
- rollout_strategy (all-at-once | canary_percent | shard)
- canary_percent (if applicable)
- change_reason (free text) and change_ticket (Linear/Jira key) — strongly preferred; if empty, include “unspecified” (don’t omit)

4) Data handling
- Do NOT log config payloads or diffs in audit logs. Metadata only. Payload/diff should remain in the config service with access controls.
- Ensure no prompt content, request bodies, or customer data appears in audit log fields.

5) Retention + export
- Default retention: 400 days in Redwood audit store.
- SIEM export: these events must be part of the export stream (same schema) and stable (schema_version increments only).

Sign-off conditions for Limited GA (Hosted/Dedicated):
- Events emitted for: create, validate, rollout start, gate failure, promote, rollback request, rollback completion.
- Each event includes the “Required fields” above (we’ll spot-check payloads).
- At least one automated test that asserts rollback emits both events and includes previous_active_version_id.
- Documentation: a short section in the customer-facing audit log docs describing these events and field meanings.

If you want, we can do a 20-min working session early next week to converge on the final taxonomy. For now, please reply with (a) what you’ll change vs defer and (b) targeted landing date for ENG-4826 / PR-19941.

— Aisha
Dr. Aisha Rahman
CISO, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 10:03 AM
Subject: Re: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions

Thanks — this is clear.

A couple clarifications so we implement what you expect:
1) For “actor_ip” and “user_agent”: do you want those for both Console-triggered actions and API-triggered actions? (Console backend can pass through request metadata; internal automation may not.)
2) For Private: we can support the same taxonomy, but the “actor_*” fields may be the operator’s identity only if they’re authenticating via the private control plane (depends on customer setup). Is it acceptable to mark actor_type=service with actor_id=installer/helm for some cases?
3) “change_ticket”: should we require format validation (e.g., LINEAR-1234 / ENG-####), or just best-effort string?

We’re trying to keep the surface area small for Limited GA but I agree rollback_requested + rollback_completed is important.

Marissa


From: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 10:21 AM
Subject: Re: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions

+1 to Marissa’s questions.

From release side: can you confirm whether audit event completeness is a hard go/no-go for enabling the rollback feature flag in prod?

If yes, I’d like to add a release train check item:
- “audit event schema_version=v1.0 present in prod logs for apply + rollback”

Also: do you care about a backfill/migration event when we convert existing (unversioned) optimization configs into versioned records?

Connor
Release Engineering Lead


From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 11:07 AM
Subject: Re: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions

Replies inline.

On Jan 10, 2026 at 10:03 AM, Marissa Cole wrote:
> 1) For “actor_ip” and “user_agent”: do you want those for both Console-triggered actions and API-triggered actions?

Yes where available. Requirement is: fields must exist; values may be “unknown” / “not_collected” for system-initiated actions. Please don’t omit the keys entirely (customers treat omission as “bug / missing audit detail”).

> 2) For Private… acceptable to mark actor_type=service with actor_id=installer/helm?

Yes. For Private deployments we should be explicit:
- actor_type=service
- actor_id=redwood-private-installer / helm-controller (or similar)
- include “operator_identity” as a single string if you have it (e.g., SSO subject), but do NOT block Private on this for Limited GA.

> 3) “change_ticket” format validation?

Best-effort string for v1.0. If validation is easy, we can warn but not reject.

On Jan 10, 2026 at 10:21 AM, Connor O'Brien wrote:
> can you confirm whether audit event completeness is a hard go/no-go for enabling the rollback feature flag in prod?

For Hosted/Dedicated Limited GA: yes, it’s a go/no-go. Rollback without a reliable audit trail is a non-starter for a chunk of enterprise prospects (and it’s a core control for change management).

> do you care about a backfill/migration event when we convert existing configs?

Yes, but it can be a separate event name and not block Limited GA if it’s clearly documented as “migration_only”. Suggested:
- optimization_config.migration_completed (include counts + time window)

Aisha


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 2:36 PM
Subject: Re: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions

Aisha — action plan proposal based on your notes (so we can align on the GA gate):

We will do for Limited GA:
- Adopt event namespace exactly as you suggested (optimization_config.*) and lock schema_version=v1.0.
- Emit: version_created, version_validated, rollout_started, rollout_gate_failed, rollout_promoted, rollback_requested, rollback_completed.
- Ensure required fields always present; when unknown we’ll populate “not_collected”.
- Add previous_active_version_id to promote + rollback_completed.
- Add a basic contract test in CI asserting both rollback events are emitted and include request_id/trace_id.
- Add doc section to the audit log page (link in your email).

We will defer (post-Limited GA):
- Detailed “gate_results” payload beyond pass/fail summary (we’ll keep pass/fail + gate_set_id for now).
- Private installer enrichment for operator identity beyond actor_type=service.

ETA:
- Target merge of audit log changes: end of next week (Fri 1/17) pending PR review.
- Release train item (Connor): add to the go/no-go checklist with a prod validation step.

Please confirm this meets your sign-off conditions.

Ava
Chief of Staff to Engineering (interim)

From: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 9:12 AM
Subject: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions
Attachments: optimization-audit-events-v1.0.docx, audit-log-payload-examples-optimization.txt

Marissa — completed security review of the Optimization Config audit event taxonomy (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback). Overall direction looks good, but we need a few changes before I can sign off for Limited GA.

Top feedback (required):

1) Event naming + lifecycle coverage
- Please standardize to a single namespace and tense. Proposed:
  - optimization_config.version_created
  - optimization_config.version_validated
  - optimization_config.rollout_started
  - optimization_config.rollout_paused
  - optimization_config.rollout_gate_failed
  - optimization_config.rollout_promoted
  - optimization_config.rollback_requested
  - optimization_config.rollback_completed
- We need BOTH “requested” and “completed” for rollback. “Completed” must reflect actual control-plane state change to prior active version (not just that an operator clicked a button).

2) Required fields (minimum) for enterprise auditability
For every event above, please ensure these are ALWAYS present (even on failures):
- event_id (uuid), event_time (RFC3339), event_name, event_schema_version
- actor_type (human | service), actor_id (user id / service id), actor_email (if human), actor_ip, user_agent (if available)
- authz_decision (allow/deny) and authz_policy_version (so we can prove RBAC enforcement)
- request_id and trace_id (or equivalent correlation IDs)
- environment (hosted | dedicated | private)
- tenant_id
- scope_model (model id/name), scope_region, scope_deployment_id (if applicable)
- config_object_id (Optimization Config object), config_version_id (immutable version), and previous_active_version_id (where applicable)
- result (success|failure) + error_code/error_summary (for failure)

3) “Blast radius” + intent
For apply/promote/rollback events, we need fields that capture what was intended:
- rollout_id
- rollout_strategy (all-at-once | canary_percent | shard)
- canary_percent (if applicable)
- change_reason (free text) and change_ticket (Linear/Jira key) — strongly preferred; if empty, include “unspecified” (don’t omit)

4) Data handling
- Do NOT log config payloads or diffs in audit logs. Metadata only. Payload/diff should remain in the config service with access controls.
- Ensure no prompt content, request bodies, or customer data appears in audit log fields.

5) Retention + export
- Default retention: 400 days in Redwood audit store.
- SIEM export: these events must be part of the export stream (same schema) and stable (schema_version increments only).

Sign-off conditions for Limited GA (Hosted/Dedicated):
- Events emitted for: create, validate, rollout start, gate failure, promote, rollback request, rollback completion.
- Each event includes the “Required fields” above (we’ll spot-check payloads).
- At least one automated test that asserts rollback emits both events and includes previous_active_version_id.
- Documentation: a short section in the customer-facing audit log docs describing these events and field meanings.

If you want, we can do a 20-min working session early next week to converge on the final taxonomy. For now, please reply with (a) what you’ll change vs defer and (b) targeted landing date for ENG-4826 / PR-19941.

— Aisha
Dr. Aisha Rahman
CISO, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 10:03 AM
Subject: Re: Audit log events for optimization config changes — security review + sign-off conditions

Thanks — this is clear.

A couple clarifications so we implement what you expect:
1) For “actor_ip” and “user_agent”: do you want those for both Console-triggered actions and API-triggered actions? (Console backend can pass through request metadata; internal automation may not.)
2) For Private: we can support the same taxonomy, but the “actor_*” fields may be the operator’s identity only if they’re authenticating via the private control plane (depends on customer setup). Is it acceptable to mark actor_type=service with actor_id=installer/helm for some cases?
3) “change_ticket”: should we require format validation (e.g., LINEAR-1234 / ENG-####), or just best-effort string?

We’re trying to keep the surface area small for Limited GA but I agree rollback_requested + rollback_completed is important.

Marissa

Also: do you care about a backfill/migration event when we convert existing (unversioned) optimization configs into versioned records?

Connor
Release Engineering Lead

Aisha

source.subject: PRD review request: Workload-aware batching defaults (fast / standard / cost-efficient tiers)
source.body:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 9:18 AM PT
Subject: PRD review request: Workload-aware batching defaults (fast / standard / cost-efficient tiers)

Hi Nikhil + Lauren — looping you in for PRD review. Ava FYI since this is part of the runtime cost optimization / 1.22 train.

Draft PRD (Confluence):
https://redwoodinference.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/PROD/pages/184221329/PRD+Batching+profiles+SLO+tiers+(Draft)

Linear tracking ticket:
PM-612 (https://linear.app/redwood/issue/PM-612/workload-aware-batching-defaults-prd)

What I’m looking for from you both (by Tue 1/14 if possible):

1) Tier semantics / customer-facing behavior
- Is the tier selected at route policy level only, or do we allow a request-level override (header/SDK param) for Hosted?
- For Hosted: if customer does nothing, do we default to “standard” or do we recommend “fast” for streaming chat routes?
- Any red flags on naming (fast/standard/cost-efficient) given customer expectations?

2) SLO targets and how we communicate them
- Are we comfortable publishing tier targets as guidance (p95/p99) vs only internal guardrails?
- For streaming: should we define separate targets for time-to-first-token vs steady-state token rate, or keep it simplified?

3) Overrides & precedence (Dedicated)
- Dedicated needs a clear override story. Proposed precedence in PRD is:
  customer override > route policy > tier default > runtime fallback.
  Please sanity check based on your roadmap / expected support load.

4) Rollout / kill switch expectations
- Hosted staged rollout: internal canary → 5% of orgs (opt-out) → 25% → 100%.
- Dedicated: explicit opt-in first.
- I’m proposing a “kill switch” that reverts to existing manual batching defaults if any tier blows p99 gates for >15m in a region.

5) Observability / “why this profile”
- Need agreement on what we show to customers in Console vs what stays internal.
- Proposed in PRD: tier label + reason codes (default/override/model-constraint/overload-mode), but no raw queue depth exposed by default.

Specific questions I’m stuck on (and would love your POV):
- Should we treat streaming endpoints as “fast” by default (seems right for support-agent/chat) OR keep standard as default to avoid surprising throughput deltas and let customers choose fast explicitly?
- How strict should we be about allowing a per-request tier selection in Hosted? (pro: experimentation; con: support/debug complexity + higher cardinality in metrics)

Thanks — please comment directly in Confluence if easier. If you’d rather do a 20 min sync, I can make time Monday afternoon.

Aditya

—
Aditya Rao
PM, Redwood Optimize
Redwood Inference


On Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 1:42 PM Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 1:42 PM PT
Subject: Re: PRD review request: Workload-aware batching defaults (fast / standard / cost-efficient tiers)

Reviewed and left comments in the PRD (mostly in the “Hosted API behavior” section).

My takes (summary here):

- Default tier for Hosted if unspecified: I strongly prefer STANDARD across the board.
  Rationale: we already have streaming routes with customer-provided prompt distributions; if we default to FAST for “streaming=true” we’ll get a support wave when customers see cost/throughput shift (even if latency improves). Better to keep predictable and message FAST as recommended for “realtime/chat UX”.

- Request-level override (header/SDK param): OK to have, but I’d frame it as “experimental” and/or feature-flagged initially.
  Concern is operational: a lot of customers will try to do per-request fast/standard toggles and then file tickets when they can’t explain tail behavior. Also metric cardinality risk if we let arbitrary tier tagging appear on every route.

- How to talk about targets: I’d avoid publishing hard p99 commitments in public docs initially. I’m fine with “expected behavior” guidance and keeping the strict gates internal (esp. early rollout).

- Naming: fast/standard/cost-efficient is fine. Avoid “realtime” (customers interpret as hard guarantee). Also please add one sentence in docs/Console copy that cost-efficient may add queueing under burst.

If helpful I can provide suggested copy for the “What happens if I don’t pick a tier?” FAQ.

Nikhil

Nikhil Sharma

Redwood Inference

title: PRD review request: Workload-aware batching defaults (fast / standard / cost-efficient tiers)
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 10, 2026 at 9:18 AM PT
Subject: PRD review request: Workload-aware batching defaults (fast / standard / cost-efficient tiers)

Hi Nikhil + Lauren — looping you in for PRD review. Ava FYI since this is part of the runtime cost optimization / 1.22 train.

Draft PRD (Confluence):
https://redwoodinference.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/PROD/pages/184221329/PRD+Batching+profiles+SLO+tiers+(Draft)

Linear tracking ticket:
PM-612 (https://linear.app/redwood/issue/PM-612/workload-aware-batching-defaults-prd)

What I’m looking for from you both (by Tue 1/14 if possible):

1) Tier semantics / customer-facing behavior
- Is the tier selected at route policy level only, or do we allow a request-level override (header/SDK param) for Hosted?
- For Hosted: if customer does nothing, do we default to “standard” or do we recommend “fast” for streaming chat routes?
- Any red flags on naming (fast/standard/cost-efficient) given customer expectations?

2) SLO targets and how we communicate them
- Are we comfortable publishing tier targets as guidance (p95/p99) vs only internal guardrails?
- For streaming: should we define separate targets for time-to-first-token vs steady-state token rate, or keep it simplified?

3) Overrides & precedence (Dedicated)
- Dedicated needs a clear override story. Proposed precedence in PRD is:
  customer override > route policy > tier default > runtime fallback.
  Please sanity check based on your roadmap / expected support load.

4) Rollout / kill switch expectations
- Hosted staged rollout: internal canary → 5% of orgs (opt-out) → 25% → 100%.
- Dedicated: explicit opt-in first.
- I’m proposing a “kill switch” that reverts to existing manual batching defaults if any tier blows p99 gates for >15m in a region.

5) Observability / “why this profile”
- Need agreement on what we show to customers in Console vs what stays internal.
- Proposed in PRD: tier label + reason codes (default/override/model-constraint/overload-mode), but no raw queue depth exposed by default.

Specific questions I’m stuck on (and would love your POV):
- Should we treat streaming endpoints as “fast” by default (seems right for support-agent/chat) OR keep standard as default to avoid surprising throughput deltas and let customers choose fast explicitly?
- How strict should we be about allowing a per-request tier selection in Hosted? (pro: experimentation; con: support/debug complexity + higher cardinality in metrics)

Thanks — please comment directly in Confluence if easier. If you’d rather do a 20 min sync, I can make time Monday afternoon.

Aditya

—
Aditya Rao
PM, Redwood Optimize
Redwood Inference

source.subject: Owner mapping dispute: capacity-related incident subtypes (Routing vs Fleet) — decision needed for v1
related_links: Linear: ENG-4825 (control-plane owner mapping config and audit)
Linear: ENG-4823 (add incident type to alert payload and pager routing)
Linear: ENG-4824 (instrument routing fallback reasons for taxonomy)
source.thread_body:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwoodinference.com>, Ravi Menon <ravi.menon@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 9, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Owner mapping dispute: capacity-related incident subtypes (Routing vs Fleet) — decision needed for v1

Sean,

We need a tiebreak decision today to unblock the v1 “incident type -> primary owner” mapping and the control-plane config (ENG-4825).

Dispute: several capacity-adjacent subtypes currently oscillate between Routing (Smart Routing) and Fleet (Infra/Fleet) as the primary owner, depending on who is on-call and what the symptom looked like.

Where it’s showing up:
- Incidents that start as “fallback storms” / elevated 5xx/429 but underlying cause is GPU pool saturation or regional capacity exhaustion.
- Incidents where router admission control sheds load (429) because the local pool is full, but routing is “doing the right thing” per policy.

Two proposed options:
A) Own by symptom: anything involving fallback/admission control defaults to Routing.
B) Own by controllable lever / root cause: capacity scarcity -> Fleet; policy/selection bugs -> Routing.

SRE recommendation is B, but Routing is concerned about being paged for “capacity incidents” and losing fast mitigation loops.

Concrete subtypes in question (from the draft sheet):
- CAPACITY > Regional capacity exhaustion
- CAPACITY > Provisioning lag / scale-up stalled
- CAPACITY > Pool saturation (GPU/utilization)
- ROUTING/FALLBACK > Excessive fallbacks due to capacity signals

Proposed owner mapping if we choose B:
- CAPACITY.* => Primary: Fleet; Secondary/Consulted: Routing (for traffic steering, safe-mode, policy constraints)
- ROUTING/FALLBACK.* => Primary: Routing; Consulted: Fleet (if capacity constraints are suspected)

Decision we need:
1) Which team is primary owner for the “capacity-driven fallback storm” subtype?
2) Can we document a single decision rule for incident commanders to pick the right primary type during an incident?

Attaching the disputed rows + the current draft mapping.

Attachment(s):
- Ownership-Mapping-v1_Disputed-Capacity-Subtypes_2026-01-09.xlsx (spreadsheet)
- incident-taxonomy-v1-owner-mapping-snippet.png (image)

Thanks,
Ava
—
Ava Chen
Platform Operations (Reliability Program)
Redwood Inference


From: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwoodinference.com>, Ravi Menon <ravi.menon@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 9, 2026 at 9:41 AM PST
Subject: Re: Owner mapping dispute: capacity-related incident subtypes (Routing vs Fleet) — decision needed for v1

+1 on needing a crisp rule, but Routing can’t end up as “consulted” on anything that is manifested as router-triggered 429/5xx.

In the last two incidents, the fastest mitigation was:
- tighten routing policy / safe-mode to reduce thrash
- force regional steering
Fleet changes (adding capacity) were slower.

If we go with B, I’d like:
- a dedicated subtype under ROUTING/FALLBACK that captures “capacity-driven fallback storms” and keeps Routing as primary
OR
- a hard rule that when fallback_reason=capacity AND traffic steering / safe-mode is required, Routing is primary.

Otherwise we’ll lose the first 30 minutes.

Mina


From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>, Ravi Menon <ravi.menon@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 9, 2026 at 10:05 AM PST
Subject: Re: Owner mapping dispute: capacity-related incident subtypes (Routing vs Fleet) — decision needed for v1

Fleet POV: primary ownership should map to who can remove the constraint.

If the router is falling back because the region/pool is out of headroom, that’s capacity. Routing mitigations can reduce blast radius, but they don’t fix the underlying shortage.

If the router is falling back incorrectly (bad health signal, bad policy rollout, wrong compatibility matrix), that’s Routing.

I’m fine with Routing being auto-paged/consulted for CAPACITY incidents (because steering matters), but the primary owner should be Fleet for CAPACITY.*

Vanessa


From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwoodinference.com>, Ravi Menon <ravi.menon@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Jan 9, 2026 at 11:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Owner mapping dispute: capacity-related incident subtypes (Routing vs Fleet) — decision needed for v1

Decision: choose option B (own by controllable boundary / root cause). We’ll treat “capacity-driven fallback storms” as a CAPACITY incident for primary ownership.

Rationale:
- Primary owner must align to the team accountable for preventing recurrence (forecasting, provisioning, pool sizing, regional capacity posture).
- Routing absolutely remains critical for mitigation; we’ll formalize it as required secondary/consulted for CAPACITY incidents.

To address Mina’s first-30-minutes concern:
- Paging/escalation rules: CAPACITY incidents should page Fleet as primary AND also notify Routing immediately when fallback/admission control is involved.
- Classification rule for ICs (write this into the decision tree):
  1) If headroom is constrained (pool saturation, provisioning backlog, regional exhaustion) OR fallback_reason=capacity is dominant -> classify as CAPACITY.* (primary Fleet).
  2) If headroom exists but router is falling back / shedding due to misconfig, bad rollout, policy bug, or incorrect health signals -> classify as ROUTING/FALLBACK.* (primary Routing).
  3) If unclear in first 15 minutes -> set CAPACITY.* with “contributing_systems=Routing” and revisit once signals confirm.

Action items (please assign owners + dates):
- Update the owner mapping table and control-plane config to reflect this (ENG-4825).
- Ensure incident-bot can set a secondary/escalation target (Routing) when CAPACITY incident includes fallback/admission symptoms (ENG-4823).

Sean


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwoodinference.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwoodinference.com>, Ravi Menon <ravi.menon@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Jan 10, 2026 at 11:28 AM PST
Subject: Re: Owner mapping dispute: capacity-related incident subtypes (Routing vs Fleet) — decision needed for v1

Thanks Sean — captured.

I’ll implement the decision in the v1 mapping + decision tree as follows:

Final mapping (v1):
- CAPACITY > Regional capacity exhaustion — Primary: Fleet; Secondary: Routing; Consulted: SRE
- CAPACITY > Provisioning lag / scale-up stalled — Primary: Fleet; Secondary: SRE; Consulted: Routing
- CAPACITY > Pool saturation (GPU/utilization) — Primary: Fleet; Secondary: Routing; Consulted: SRE
- ROUTING/FALLBACK > Fallback misbehavior (policy/health/compat bugs) — Primary: Routing; Secondary: SRE; Consulted: Fleet

IC decision rule (one-liner for the bot + policy page):
- “If the constraint is headroom (saturation/backlog/exhaustion) -> CAPACITY (Fleet owns). If headroom exists but fallbacks/shedding are incorrect -> ROUTING/FALLBACK (Routing owns). If unknown -> CAPACITY + tag Routing as contributing system, revisit within 30 minutes.”

Follow-ups (next week):
1) Ava: update Confluence owner mapping + decision tree language and link it from the incident classification policy. ETA Tue 1/13.
2) Rafael: confirm escalation rule wording for CAPACITY incidents (Fleet primary + Routing notified) and align with incident-bot closure gating. ETA Wed 1/14.
3) Mina/Vanessa: provide 2–3 concrete examples each for the taxonomy page (what to pick, what not to pick). ETA Wed 1/14.
4) Routing team: ensure fallback_reason enums land + are visible in the incident channel bot summary (ENG-4824). Tracking separately.

If anyone thinks we need a dedicated “Capacity-driven fallback storm” subtype name for readability, we can add it as an alias in the UI copy while keeping the underlying type low-cardinality (CAPACITY > saturation/exhaustion) for metrics.

Ava
—
Ava Chen
Platform Operations (Reliability Program)
Redwood Inference

Linear: ENG-4823 (add incident type to alert payload and pager routing)
Linear: ENG-4824 (instrument routing fallback reasons for taxonomy)

source.subject: Exec approval: Enterprise priority semantics + hot-route protection rollout (Hosted API)
source.body:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Dec 6, 2025 at 9:14 AM PST
Subject: Exec approval: Enterprise priority semantics + hot-route protection rollout (Hosted API)
Attachments: Customer_Comms_Draft_Priority_Routing_Quotas_v3.docx; HotRoute_Protection_Rollout_Risk_Assessment_v2.pdf

Neha,

Requesting exec approval to proceed with the enterprise-priority semantics for Hot-route Capacity Protection and to authorize the staged rollout plan (us-east canary -> multi-region).

Decision needed:
1) Customer-facing semantics: We will explicitly treat enterprise traffic on protected routes (/v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings, /v1/rerank) as “priority class” with reserved per-route/per-tier budgets.
2) Overload behavior: Under pressure, best-effort/self-serve traffic will be throttled first (429) and may be shed earlier; enterprise should preserve SLOs until enterprise budgets are exhausted.
3) Messaging: We plan to communicate as “capacity protection to maintain stability” (not “paid fast lane”), with clear 429 reason codes + retry guidance.

Risk assessment summary (full detail in attached PDF):
- Risk: 429 spikes trigger customer confusion/escalations (esp. during rollout). Mitigation: consistent 429 payload/headers + support macros; staged allowlist; canary guardrails.
- Risk: “silent” quality changes if fallback to alt model/region. Mitigation: for enterprise routes, fallback is opt-in and limited by route guardrails; otherwise strict admission control only.
- Risk: added data plane latency from policy evaluation. Mitigation: cached/batched lookups; SLA budgeted overhead <1ms p95 at gateway.
- Risk: alerting noise/paging storms. Mitigation: burn-rate + sustained trigger alerts; no paging on expected best-effort shedding.

Rollout plan:
- Dec 10–12: us-east canary (allowlisted enterprise accounts only), 1% -> 10% -> 50% traffic on protected routes.
- Week of Dec 15: us-west + eu-west, expand allowlist.
- GA target: end of Dec, contingent on canary success metrics.

Success criteria (canary):
- Enterprise protected routes: no regression in p95 latency; reduced 5xx during induced load tests; observed admission control activation w/ stable enterprise.
- Best-effort: 429 is predictable w/ correct reason codes; no gateway error spikes.

Asks:
- Can you approve the semantics + rollout plan as outlined?
- Any redlines on the customer comms framing (attached doc)?

Thanks,
Ava

—
Ava Chen
Redwood Inference



From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Dec 6, 2025 at 11:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec approval: Enterprise priority semantics + hot-route protection rollout (Hosted API)

Thanks Ava.

Before I sign off, I need clarity on two points:

1) “Enterprise budgets exhausted” — what happens in that state? Is it 429 with a distinct reason code vs 503? And are we committing to any “never throttle enterprise” language in comms?

2) Any scenario where best-effort shedding could cascade into enterprise impact (shared GPU pools, head-of-line blocking)? If yes, what’s the explicit backpressure policy and what signals trigger it?

Also, please confirm Support readiness: do we have an escalation path + exception process documented for burst-limit asks?

-Neha



From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Dec 6, 2025 at 1:27 PM PST
Subject: Re: Exec approval: Enterprise priority semantics + hot-route protection rollout (Hosted API)
Attachments: Launch_Checklist_Hot_Route_Protection_US_East_Canary.xlsx

Neha,

Answers inline:

1) Enterprise budgets exhausted behavior
- Response: 429 (not 503) for admission-control denies, with consistent schema + headers.
- Reason codes:
  - quota_exceeded (standard)
  - rate_limited (standard)
  - overload_protection (new; means fleet/route pressure)
  - enterprise_route_budget_exhausted (new; indicates the enterprise-reserved budget is depleted)
- Comms: we are not using “never throttle enterprise” anywhere. Draft language: “Enterprise tiers are prioritized to maintain stability during fleet pressure; requests may still be rate limited if contractual or configured limits are exceeded.”

2) Preventing best-effort -> enterprise cascade
- The main failure mode we’re addressing is best-effort bursts driving queue depth/scheduler lag so high that enterprise tail latency and 5xx increase.
- Policy: gate best-effort earlier using capacity signals (queue depth + scheduler lag) + per-route admission control. Once triggers trip, best-effort is throttled and/or shed; enterprise continues to be admitted until enterprise budget or hard safety thresholds are hit.
- Signals/triggers: we’re standardizing on queue depth + scheduler lag over raw GPU utilization (less noisy). Trigger is sustained threshold over N seconds (avoids flapping). On trigger, routing moves best-effort to “deny unless under baseline” and preserves enterprise budgets.

Support readiness + exceptions
- Yes: Support has an escalation path for burst-limit asks. The planned workflow is: Support -> TAM/CS -> Solutions -> Platform on-call for temp exception (time-bounded + guardrails) with logging.
- We’re not changing billing; exceptions are policy config only.

I attached the canary launch checklist (owner names + go/no-go gates). If you want, I can add a specific “enterprise budget exhausted” comms snippet to the draft for clarity.

-Ava



From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Dec 7, 2025 at 8:41 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec approval: Enterprise priority semantics + hot-route protection rollout (Hosted API)

This is directionally good.

Two conditions for approval:

A) Comms must avoid the term “priority class” externally. Use “capacity protection” / “stability protections” language. Internally we can call it priority.

B) Add an explicit internal-only note that if enterprise hard safety thresholds trip (e.g., queue depth beyond safe operation), we may deny enterprise with 429 and “overload_protection” and we should page SRE immediately. I want that to be part of the operational contract.

If you can confirm those are addressed, I’m comfortable approving the us-east allowlisted canary.

-Neha



From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Dec 7, 2025 at 2:16 PM PST
Subject: Re: Exec approval: Enterprise priority semantics + hot-route protection rollout (Hosted API)

Confirmed on both conditions.

A) Customer comms: we’ll remove/avoid “priority class” terminology. We’ll position as “stability protections for enterprise routes” and focus on predictable 429 behavior + retry guidance.

B) Operational contract: agreed. We’ll document that if hard safety thresholds trip, enterprise can be denied with 429 + overload_protection, and that condition triggers paging + incident-bot notification. (We’ll keep “enterprise_route_budget_exhausted” reserved for normal budget depletion; hard safety is a separate overload path.)

I’ll update the comms draft + checklist today and send you the final versions.

-Ava



From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, Dec 8, 2025 at 4:02 PM PST
Subject: Re: Exec approval: Enterprise priority semantics + hot-route protection rollout (Hosted API)

Approved for the us-east allowlisted canary under the conditions we discussed.

Please make sure we have:
- clear rollback criteria (latency regression, 5xx increase, unexpected enterprise denies)
- Support/CS briefed before the first enablement window
- a short post-canary readout (24h + 72h) with enterprise SLO impact + shed metrics

Thanks,
Neha

—
Neha Kapoor
Exec Sponsor (Reliability)
Redwood Inference

source.subject: Re: Urgent: Streaming responses stalling on Redwood API
source.body:
From: Nina Patel <nina.patel@northstar-analytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 7, 2025 at 4:18 PM UTC
Subject: Urgent: Streaming responses stalling on Redwood API

Hi Ava,

Were seeing our streaming chat responses stall mid-output. The HTTP connection stays open but tokens stop coming. We dont receive a terminal done event, so the UI looks like its hanging.

Example request IDs (prod us-east):
- rwreq_9b2f0c8c (2025-11-07 16:02 UTC)
- rwreq_1a77d3e1 (2025-11-07 15:57 UTC)

Can you advise?

Thanks,
Nina

---

From: Avery Johnson <avery@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nina Patel <nina.patel@northstar-analytics.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 7, 2025 at 5:01 PM UTC
Subject: Re: Urgent: Streaming responses stalling on Redwood API

Thanks Nina  were investigating and looping in SRE + runtime engineering. Well update shortly.

---

From: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
To: Nina Patel <nina.patel@northstar-analytics.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 7, 2025 at 6:12 PM UTC
Subject: Re: Urgent: Streaming responses stalling on Redwood API

Nina  acknowledged. Were tracking this under SUP-1842. Symptom matches an internal streaming stall were mitigating with a runtime patch in progress.

In the meantime, if you can tolerate it, switching to non-streaming for long outputs avoids the hang.

---

From: Nina Patel <nina.patel@northstar-analytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 7, 2025 at 7:44 PM UTC
Subject: Re: Urgent: Streaming responses stalling on Redwood API

Got it. We can temporarily disable streaming for the affected route. Please keep us posted on the patch timing.

Date: Fri, Nov 7, 2025 at 6:12 PM UTC
Subject: Re: Urgent: Streaming responses stalling on Redwood API

Nina  acknowledged. Were tracking this under SUP-1842. Symptom matches an internal streaming stall were mitigating with a runtime patch in progress.

In the meantime, if you can tolerate it, switching to non-streaming for long outputs avoids the hang.

---

From: Nina Patel <nina.patel@northstar-analytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Nov 7, 2025 at 7:44 PM UTC
Subject: Re: Urgent: Streaming responses stalling on Redwood API

Got it. We can temporarily disable streaming for the affected route. Please keep us posted on the patch timing.

source.subject: PII pseudonymization & on-prem access diagram review
messages:
From: Laura Simpson <laura@medicore.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: MediCore Security Team <security@medicore.com>
Date: 2025-09-15T09:22:11-07:00
Subject: PII pseudonymization & on-prem access diagram review

Hi Ava,

Thanks for taking this on. As part of MediCore's DPA annex review we're looking to confirm how Redwood will handle pseudonymization and access controls for our on-prem inference deployment. Specifically we need:

- A technical mapping of which fields you consider PII vs derived identifiers (name, MRN, device id, free-text notes).
- The pseudonymization approach (one-way hash, tokenization, reversible encryption) and where re-identification keys (if any) are stored.
- Network segmentation / access diagram showing which components are in-customer VPC vs Redwood-managed control plane, and where logs/audit trails are retained.
- Roles who can access re-identification (if applicable) and evidence of KMS/HSM integration.

Can you share a diagram and a short mapping spreadsheet by EOD Wednesday (9/17)? If there's anything you need from us (e.g., sample schema or classification tags) let me know.

Best,
Laura Simpson
Senior Privacy Counsel
MediCore Health

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Laura Simpson <laura@medicore.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-09-15T11:08:34-07:00
Subject: Re: PII pseudonymization & on-prem access diagram review

Hi Laura,

Thanks — we can absolutely provide that. Attaching a working spreadsheet that maps the most common fields we see and our recommended pseudonymization pattern. High-level summary below; the attached draft has row-level notes per field.

Proposal (draft):
- Data classification: We classify explicit identifiers (name, MRN, SSN) as PII; device IDs and session tokens as persistent identifiers; free-text notes are treated as sensitive unstructured text.
- Pseudonymization approach: one-way salted hashing for identifiers used only for analytics (non-reversible); deterministic tokenization for cases where cross-session matching is required. Deterministic tokens are generated using a per-customer salt and a keyed tokenization service.
- Re-identification keys: for deterministic tokenization we do not store plaintext mapping in Redwood. Instead, the tokenization key is stored in the customer's KMS (recommended) or in an HSM-backed Redwood KMS instance for Dedicated/Private deployments. Redwood personnel do not have direct access to KMS plaintext without an approved CustOps playbook and two-person authorization.
- Access controls & audit: RBAC + scoped service accounts, hourly audit log export to customer-controlled S3, and event-level logging of any key unwrap operations.
- Network segmentation: for Private deployments, inference nodes sit in the customer VPC and only the agent/control-plane has outbound to Redwood for control messages. See attached network diagram (v1).

Attachments: draft-pseudonymization-mapping.xlsx (spreadsheet)

Next steps: Jonas — can you and Marissa review the proposed wording around tokenization, KMS custody, and the access approval flow? We'll incorporate any legal/security redlines before I send a cleaned version to MediCore.

Laura — can you confirm whether MediCore requires the re-identification key to be exclusively customer-managed KMS (preferred) or if a dedicated HSM instance managed by Redwood is acceptable subject to contract language?

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
(415) 555-0193

From: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2025-09-16T10:12:47-07:00
Subject: Re: PII pseudonymization & on-prem access diagram review

Ava — quick technical review and a couple of suggested changes before this goes to the customer. Inline below.

On Sep 15, 2025, at 11:08 AM, Ava Chen wrote:
> Proposal (draft):
> - Data classification: We classify explicit identifiers (name, MRN, SSN) as PII; device IDs and session tokens as persistent identifiers; free-text notes are treated as sensitive unstructured text.

Suggestion: add a small note about derived features (e.g., embeddings) — treat as pseudonymized but include a retention TTL and forbid export without attestation.

> - Pseudonymization approach: one-way salted hashing for identifiers used only for analytics (non-reversible); deterministic tokenization for cases where cross-session matching is required. Deterministic tokens are generated using a per-customer salt and a keyed tokenization service.

Suggestion: for deterministic tokenization used in clinical matching, prefer format-preserving tokenization on input to reduce fingerprinting risk. Also call out rotation policy for salts/keys and mention a fallback strategy for token collisions.

> - Re-identification keys: for deterministic tokenization we do not store plaintext mapping in Redwood. Instead, the tokenization key is stored in the customer's KMS (recommended) or in an HSM-backed Redwood KMS instance for Dedicated/Private deployments. Redwood personnel do not have direct access to KMS plaintext without an approved CustOps playbook and two-person authorization.

Confirm: we should be explicit that CustOps approvals are recorded in an immutable audit trail and that any emergency unseal requires both a legal & security ticket. I've attached a redline of the network diagram (network-segmentation-v2.png) with a clearer depiction of the control-plane hop and the audit export path. Also adding a short KMS attestation template (kms-attestation-redacted.txt) that we can provide under NDA.

I'll also flag Marissa for any contractual language around 'exclusive customer KMS' vs 'Redwood HSM with contractual guarantees'.

Attachments: network-segmentation-v2.png, kms-attestation-redacted.txt

-Jonas
Jonas Weber
Principal Security Engineer, Redwood Inference
jonas.weber@redwood.com

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Laura Simpson <laura@medicore.com>
Cc: MediCore Security Team <security@medicore.com>
Date: 2025-09-17T14:03:05-07:00
Subject: Re: PII pseudonymization & on-prem access diagram review

Hi Laura — attaching the consolidated materials and suggested contractual language excerpts per your ask. We've incorporated security feedback (see network segmentation v2) and outlined the custody options for re-identification keys. Summary:

1) Field mapping: attached spreadsheet maps common schema elements and our recommended transformation (one-way hash vs deterministic token). See spreadsheet rows for notes where free-text requires additional redaction.

2) Deterministic tokenization: supported for cross-session matching. Tokenization keys SHOULD be customer-managed in KMS. If MediCore prefers Redwood-managed HSM for Dedicated/Private, we can provide an HSM-backed key under a contractual attestation and two-person CustOps approval flow.

3) Access & audit: RBAC for service accounts, two-person approval for any key unseal, hourly audit export to customer-controlled storage, and immutable audit trail for approval events.

4) Network: inference nodes reside in the customer VPC. Control-plane communicates outbound only over mTLS to Redwood endpoints; audit exports flow to a customer S3 bucket in the same region (see network-segmentation-v2.png).

Attachments (for review):
- draft-pseudonymization-mapping.xlsx (spreadsheet)
- network-segmentation-v2.png (diagram)
- kms-attestation-redacted.txt (attestation template, redacted; full available under NDA)

Action items:
- Laura: please confirm whether exclusive customer KMS is required or if Redwood-managed HSM (with attestation) is acceptable.
- MediCore security: if you want any further detail on rotation policies or collision handling for deterministic tokens, tell us which artifacts (rotation cadence, deterministic algorithm spec) you prefer and we'll include.

If everything looks good we'll add the agreed language to the DPA annex and circulate to procurement for signature.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
(415) 555-0193

---
Attachments referenced above are included as stubs in this thread: draft-pseudonymization-mapping.xlsx; network-segmentation-v2.png; kms-attestation-redacted.txt
